findings.title: Missing signer check on vault authority — any account can drain vault funds
findings.attack_path: Attacker calls withdraw() with an arbitrary authority account that is not a signer. The ix macro does not enforce `#[account(signer)]` on vault_authority, so any pubkey can be passed. Attacker drains entire vault balance in a single transaction.
findings.notes: Confirmed critical. The account constraint is missing `Signer<'info>` type or `#[account(signer)]`. The `require!` check only validates the pubkey matches — not that the transaction was signed by that key. This is a classic Anchor signer check vulnerability that would allow complete fund drainage.

findings.title: Integer overflow in reward calculation — unchecked multiplication on u64
findings.attack_path: If `stake_amount` is close to u64::MAX and `reward_rate` > 1, the multiplication on line 142 wraps to a small value, allowing the attacker to claim near-zero rewards while the protocol believes a full payout occurred. Can be triggered by depositing a crafted amount.
findings.notes: High confidence. Should use checked_mul() or saturating_mul(). The overflow causes reward underpayment — attacker can craft stake amounts to manipulate reward accounting and drain the reward pool over time.

findings.title: CVE-2024-43370 in curve25519-dalek 3.x — timing side-channel in scalar multiplication
findings.immunefi_impact: High — Key extraction via timing attack
findings.attack_path: curve25519-dalek 3.x (used transitively via solana-program 1.17.x) has a known timing side-channel in its scalar multiplication. An attacker with precise timing measurement can recover private key material after ~1000 observed operations.
findings.notes: Definite CVE. Upgrade curve25519-dalek to 4.1.3 or pin solana-program to >=1.18.x which pulls in the fixed version. CVSS 7.4 (High).

findings.title: PDA derivation uses user-controlled seed without domain separation
findings.attack_path: The PDA is derived with `[b"vault", user.key().as_ref(), vault_id.to_le_bytes()]`. If vault_id is user-controlled, a malicious user can craft a vault_id that collides with another user's PDA and overwrite their account state on initialization.
findings.notes: Medium confidence. The collision is only possible if vault_id is user-supplied without bounds checking. Recommend adding program-controlled nonce or using the user's account creation count as part of the seed.

findings.title: GitHub Actions CI/CD secret exposure via unsanitized `github.event` context
findings.attack_path: The deploy workflow interpolates `${{ github.event.pull_request.head.repo.full_name }}` directly into a `run:` step. A malicious PR from a fork could exfiltrate DEPLOY_KEYPAIR and PROGRAM_ID secrets by injecting shell commands.
findings.code_context:
- name: Deploy program
  run: |
    anchor deploy --program-name ${{ github.event.inputs.program }} \
      --provider.cluster $CLUSTER
  env:
    DEPLOY_KEYPAIR: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_KEYPAIR }}
findings.notes: Confirmed. Use an intermediate env var and restrict workflow trigger to protected branches only. The secrets would allow deploying a malicious program upgrade.
